summary: This task fit cleanly as an emitter-owned control-plane manifest addition; reusing the existing control-plane runtime demo avoided a parser/checker expansion and still gave end-to-end coverage.
ergonomics: The generated manifest pattern in `Clasp.Emit.JavaScript` makes it easy to add a new compiler-owned surface that automatically shows up in bindings and docs.
The existing control-plane runtime scenario is a strong verification anchor because it already exercises policies, secrets, hooks, tools, and traceability in one path.
Large emitted-JavaScript string blocks remain brittle: small feature changes require careful synchronized updates across emitter strings and exact substring assertions.
follow_ups: Add source-level audit-log declarations once control-plane syntax is ready so sink names and retention values are not compiler-hardcoded defaults.
Unify emitted audit records onto a single typed envelope instead of parallel ad hoc object shapes tagged with `eventType`.
Extend the audit runtime helper to enforce age-based retention and, if needed, durable sink adapters beyond the current in-memory runtime.
warnings: Targeted Haskell tests still need `nix develop` in this workspace because `ghc` is not available on the host PATH.
The audit redaction helper only scrubs a fixed sensitive-key list right now; new secret-bearing field names will not be redacted until that list is updated.
Routing is shape-driven rather than declaration-driven today, so editing emitted event fields without checking the router can silently move entries to the wrong sink.
tests_run: nix develop -c bash -lc "cabal test clasp-compiler-test --test-options='-p control-plane'"
residual_risks: `maxAgeDays` is exported as retention metadata but the new in-memory audit runtime only enforces entry-count trimming today.

Audit routing is currently inferred from existing emitted event shapes and `eventType` tags, so future shape changes in policy/secret/traceability records could require matching updates in the routing helper.
